module_mood_contract: learning through contrast — 'I can feel what packaging adds now; I couldn't have read it as a lecture.' Pre-read function: prime the diagnostic lens and plant the question without resolving it. Mood at pre-read close: curious and primed, 8/10.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood lands (per phase + close): mood_score 8+/10 at every teaching beat.
rules_evaluated.evidence: trace.phases[1].mood_score=8, trace.close.mood_score=8. Single teaching beat (whole-body lecture, no Phase headers). Beat is TEACHING (plants failure-mode vocabulary + diagnostic question) — mood-floor applies. Score 8 in both pre-read and in-room mode. Persona leaves 'curious and primed,' not anxious or corrected.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood-floor carve-out: setup/launch beats are mood-exempt.
rules_evaluated.evidence: No setup/launch beats in this lecture. All content is teaching-mode.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Mood doesn't resolve early: this file must not preempt M5's teaching moment.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Line 23-24: 'He had something specific in place that catches the failure modes above. M5 diagnoses what.' — the resolution is explicitly withheld. The three-pattern (reference artifact + plan.md + verifier) is named nowhere in this file. Maintainer watch-for confirms: 'Don't pre-empt the three-pattern.' Persona traces the itch of wanting the answer without getting it — correct design intent.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Teaching moment lands: named teaching moment reliably triggers across reasonable persona-skill variation.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Three failure modes (goal drift, context rot, plausible-but-wrong) each get a dedicated paragraph with practitioner-grounded descriptions. The diagnostic question ('What would have caught this at hour 2, not hour 6?') is bolded and positioned before the failure-mode list. Reliable across pre-read and in-room modes. Even a fast reader absorbs all three names — the list is short, concrete, and non-overlapping. One confusion flag (context rot / context-window conflation) is non-blocking.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Big Idea fidelity: file honors its mood contract and M5 Big Idea framing.
rules_evaluated.evidence: M5 Big Idea: 'Read the un-packaged Module 4 run through three failure-mode lenses, build the validation that would have caught each.' This lecture delivers exactly the lenses (failure modes) and frames the diagnostic question without delivering the prescription. File opens: 'Here is the question that drives M5.' — directly honors the Big Idea's diagnostic spirit. No prescription, no three-pattern, no 'best practice' register (per maintainer watch-for).

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Module-to-module arc: this file's close hands off to M5 open.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Lines 29: 'M5 opens with reading it together.' — explicit forward hand-off. Pre-read mode: student arrives at M5 with the vocabulary seated, ready to read their artefact. The 'What to bring to M5' section (lines 26-29) names exactly what M5's Connections + in-room opener expects. Clean hand-off.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Body teaches the work; trainer manages the room outside text.
rules_evaluated.evidence: No authored conversation cues, no 'share with your neighbor,' no pause cues, no 'take 90 seconds' beats. The body is about the work: the artefact, the three failure modes, the Ronacher anchor. The dual-mode use (pre-read + in-room) requires no trainer cue in body — the lecture is short enough that projection + silent read works without instruction. PASS against check_student_facing.md rule 26 hard-grep list as well.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Forcing functions live in prompts, not body prose.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This is a lecture with no fenced prompt blocks. The diagnostic question ('What would have caught this at hour 2, not hour 6?') is framing prose appropriate to a pre-read lecture — it is not a forcing function that should live in a prompt. N/A.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Meta-frame lectures are closers, not openers.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This lecture is NOT a meta-frame lecture (it does not name a pattern the student just lived through). It is a PRIMING lecture: it introduces failure-mode vocabulary and plants the diagnostic question before the student does the work. Per check_lectures.md taxonomy: 'Demo lectures showing a ceiling go at the beginning; conceptual lectures introducing vocabulary land mid-module where the vocab earns itself.' This is a vocabulary-introduction pre-read, placed correctly before the M5 exercise. PASS.

Pre-read carries: spirit (diagnostic question), lenses (three failure modes in one paragraph each), one practitioner anchor (Ronacher with token count — grounding, not framework delivery). Does NOT carry: the three-pattern fix, named practitioner terms for the solutions, operational primitives, tradeoff explanations. Within the dosage ceiling for a contrast-mood opener. The in-room opener (learning-through-contrast.md) handles the named-operational-primitive (manual /compact at ~60%) and the FAQ; this lecture stays lean.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Epistemic mood is not punitive mood.
rules_evaluated.evidence: The unease (if any) is diagnostic and curious: 'What would have caught this?' — not corrective. No language of blame, failure-as-student-fault, or shame. The three failure modes are named as engineering phenomena, not student errors. Maintainer push-back moves confirm: 'the un-packaged run was supposed to underdeliver. The diagnosis is data, not blame.' The pre-read honors this: failure modes are systemic, not personal.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Lead with the discipline, not the failure mode.
rules_evaluated.evidence: This lecture's job IS to name failure modes (they are the diagnostic lens the student needs). But the framing leads with the discipline: the diagnostic question ('What would have caught this?') is stated first and in bold before the failure-mode list. Failure modes are tools for the diagnostic move, not the title of the move. The discipline named: 'you read the artefact, you find what went wrong, and for each thing you ask: what would have caught this earlier?' — the practice leads, the taxonomy follows. PASS.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Dual-mode reader UX: student can follow the lecture in both pre-read and in-room modes without hitting a 'wait, what?' moment.
rules_evaluated.evidence: Pre-read mode: ~370 words, 5-min read, standalone. No references that assume M5 has already started. No fenced prompts or session widgets. In-room mode: trainer projects, room reads cold (2-3 min) or refreshes from pre-read. No authored trainer cue needed — short enough for silent projection read. Dual-mode concern from pre-cohort-todos is resolved by the lecture's brevity and self-containedness. One trainer-discretion gap noted (in-room readers who skipped pre-read may benefit from a verbal one-liner from the trainer) — not a body-text fix, not a rule violation.
